preFix.title: Surface sizing for watercolour
preFix.subtitle: Applying a warm gelatine size to handmade or unsized paper to close the surface and make it suitable for watercolour washes
preFix.excerpt: An unsized handmade sheet absorbs watercolour like blotting paper, pigment sinks in instantly, colours separate and feather, and washes cannot be lifted. A thin coat of warm gelatine size closes the surface fibres without sealing them completely, producing a sheet that behaves like traditional cold-press watercolour paper: slow absorbency, clean washes, and the ability to rework wet areas.
preFix.sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (2nd ed., 1947), chapter on sizing. Internet Archive.

content.text: is applied hot to a cold, completely dry sheet. The gelatine is liquid when warm and gels on the paper as it cools, depositing a thin protein film that binds the surface fibres together. The correct amount, a barely visible sheen, not a glossy coat, stops
without making the surface impermeable to watercolour.

items.name: pH testing strips (to verify neutral size)

content.text: Soak 3-4 leaves of 250-bloom platinum gelatine in cold water for 5 minutes until softened.

Squeeze out excess water and place in the top pan of a double boiler with 500 ml of warm water. Heat gently, stirring, until the gelatine dissolves completely. Do not boil, sustained heat degrades the protein and weakens the size.
Check the temperature: the size should be applied at 45-50°C, hot enough to stay liquid and flow easily but not so hot it warps the dry sheet.

Lay the dry sheet flat on a clean board. Load the 50 mm flat brush with size and brush in one direction across the sheet, working quickly from one edge to the other. The size should coat the surface in a single pass, going over an area a second time before it sets can pull and damage the surface fibres. Size both sides for full watercolour capability; sizing one side only gives an intermediate result and can cause unequal tension warping.
A simple alternative to brush sizing is

: fill a tray with warm size solution, lower the sheet onto the surface, let it absorb for 10 seconds, then lift and hang it to dry. Tub sizing coats both sides at once and gives a more even result on sheets with an uneven surface.

items.fix: Size both faces in quick succession, or tub-size the sheet so both faces receive the solution at the same time.
items.cause: Both faces of the sheet absorbed the size at different rates, causing differential expansion and contraction.
items.symptom: The sized sheet cockles badly when drying

items.fix: Apply a second coat of size after the first is dry. Ensure the base sheet is completely dry and at room temperature before sizing, a sheet that feels cold to the back of the hand still contains moisture.
items.cause: The size solution was too dilute, or the sheet was not completely dry before sizing.
items.symptom: Watercolour still sinks and feathers on the sized sheet
